summary: The Japanese Presbyterian Conference sponsored the project I was involved in. As a minister, I was connected to this organization, which supported our community activities. Their involvement helped give structure and support to our efforts during that time.

My name is Tokushige Kizuka, born in 1901 in Fukuoka Prefecture during the 34th year of Meiji. I came to America at age 16 to join my father who was managing an apple orchard in Watsonville, California. My early years were marked by separation from my parents and adjusting to life in the United States. I worked hard in the orchards and lived through many challenges as a young immigrant.

I was born in Fukuoka Prefecture, Japan, and spent my childhood there before coming to America. It was a rural area where I attended elementary and middle school, and I remember the local customs and my teachers well. Many people from my village later emigrated, and I knew several who also ended up in Watsonville and other places abroad.

I was born in 1901, which was the 34th year of the Meiji period. This era was a time of great change in Japan, with modernization and new ideas spreading. My father left for America during this period, around the 38th year of Meiji, which shaped much of my early family history.

My father fought in the Russo-Japanese War and returned victorious. I remember the celebration when he came back, including a long parade where I was carried with a big drum on a handcart. That event was a proud moment for our family and community.

One of my elementary school teachers, named Tokan, was sent to Taiwan because of his advanced and new ideas. He was a prodigy and someone I respected greatly, even though his progressive thoughts were not accepted in Japan at the time. Taiwan was where he continued his teaching after leaving our school.

I boarded the ship Tenyo Maru in Nagasaki to come to America when I was 16. The ship ran aground near Yokosuka shortly after leaving Nagasaki, so we had to stay on board for about a week before continuing. Nagasaki was the last port I saw in Japan before my long journey.

I traveled to America on the ship Tenyo Maru, which left from Nagasaki. The ship ran aground near Yokosuka, causing a delay of about a week, but it was repaired and continued to Kobe, Honolulu, and then San Francisco. Life on the ship was interesting, with many picture brides and returnees sharing stories.

After leaving Nagasaki, the Tenyo Maru ran aground near Yokosuka, which delayed our journey by about a week. We stayed on the ship during this time while divers inspected the hull. Yokosuka was an unexpected stop on my voyage to America.

After the delay near Yokosuka, the Tenyo Maru continued to Yokohama before heading overseas. Yokohama was one of the last Japanese ports we stopped at before crossing the Pacific. It was part of the route that took me from Japan to America.

The ship stopped at Kobe after leaving Yokohama on the way to Honolulu. Kobe was one of the ports on the route from Japan to America. It was part of the journey that took me across the Pacific Ocean to start my new life.

We stayed overnight in Honolulu during our voyage to America. The returnees who spoke English took us sightseeing around town and treated us well. I remember trying to buy bananas with 50 cents, but they were still green and we couldn’t bring them on board due to American regulations.

We arrived at San Francisco after about 17 days at sea, including the delay near Yokosuka. At the Immigration Office on Angel Island, we underwent thorough physical examinations before entering the United States. This was the first place I set foot on American soil after leaving Japan.

We stayed at Angel Island for about 3 to 4 days during immigration processing. I wasn’t afraid because I was with friends, but it was a strict and thorough examination place. It was where I officially entered the United States after my long journey.

My father was managing an apple orchard in Watsonville, California, when I arrived. I worked for him there, starting with pruning and other orchard tasks. Watsonville was where I first experienced life in America and the hard work that came with it.

I came to the United States at age 16 to join my father who had been living here for years. America was a big country and life here was hard work, especially in the orchards. I lived in California and experienced many challenges adjusting to a new culture and environment.

At the time I was growing up in Japan, the government was encouraging people to emigrate to South America. Although I never had a definite plan, many from my area considered that option before I was summoned by my father to come to America. South America was often mentioned as a destination for emigrants then.
